Paragraph_1_Text: China's high-tech surge isn't without challenges. Quality sometimes lags behind quantity, indigenous innovation battles with technology copying accusations, and geopolitical tensions threaten access to global markets and collaborations. Brain drain remains an issue as top talent seeks opportunities abroad.
Paragraph_2_Text: China's high-tech surge isn't without challenges. Quality sometimes lags behind quantity, indigenous innovation battles with technology copying accusations, and geopolitical tensions threaten access to global markets and collaborations. Brain drain remains an issue as top talent seeks opportunities abroad.

Paragraph_1_Text: Yet the momentum is undeniable. With over 3 million STEM graduates annually, R&D spending exceeding 2.5% of GDP, and a government committed to technological self-reliance, China is positioned to shape the 21st century's technological landscape. The question is no longer whether China can innovate at the highest levels—it's how the world will respond to a new technology superpower.
Paragraph_2_Text: Yet the momentum is undeniable. Paragraph_1_Text: From quantum computers to space stations, from AI to green energy, China's recent technological advances represent more than national achievement. They signal a fundamental shift in global innovation geography, one that will define opportunities and challenges for decades to come.
